meta.title: What Wall Street Wants: Metrics That Matter

transcript: Hey everyone. Uh, we're gonna talk about how do we model Solana. How should we value Solana? Uh, the talk is What Wall Street Wants: What are the metrics that actually matter for Solana, for valuing Solana. Uh, so a quick background on us. So Artemis, we are building Bloomberg, CapIQ for those from TradFi, CapIQ for digital finance. Um, I sold my soul and worked in TradFi. I was a venture capitalist back in 2014, investing in software companies. And then most recently I was at a hedge fund investing in fintech, crypto, and software. And, um, today I'm really proud to say Artemis, we back the largest asset managers in the world. Uh, our customers are like Fidelity, T. Rowe, VanEck, Franklin Templeton. And I'm about to share with you a really big problem, which is Solana trades like oil and gas. It trades like a commodity. Solana trades like, based off of supply and demand, based off of flows. Unlike a high-growth technology asset, which is why I got into crypto. I wanted to invest in the next big high-growth technology business. Solana doesn't trade like Nvidia where when data center revenue goes up, Nvidia stock price goes up. And I literally did this at my last company. I worked in venture. I was like, okay, what are the key metrics that matter for software and technology businesses? Let's commoditize it, let's turn it into a product. And our customers were Sequoia, Benchmark, Tiger Global, Altimeter, et cetera. And that's the mission behind Artemis. What's the Rule of 40? What's the ARR equivalent for blockchains? Like, how the heck do we value these Layer 1s, these L2s, and these protocols? And our team comes from BlackRock, Citadel, Ribbit, um, et cetera. So here's the key learning that we realized over the last four years. TVL, developer activity, daily active addresses, transactions do not matter to Wall Street. It does not matter for valuation. We tried that. We had market cap to TVL, we had market cap to daily active addresses. That was one of the core metrics that we made comparable. They don't move markets. It just doesn't matter. So today I'm gonna propose what we can do to solve that problem so Solana doesn't trade like oil and gas and trades like a high-growth technology asset. So taking a step back, like what actually works in crypto today? BNB is one of the top performing tokens this year. Revenue was up 3x, price, market cap was up roughly 3x. Hyperliquid. Everyone loves Hyperliquid. Hyperliquid's revenue was up roughly two and a half X and price was up 2x earlier this fall. And in the equities market, everyone loves Robinhood. Robinhood revenue is up like 50% year on year and stock price is ripping. And in 2025, like the three tokens that are positive, that actually had a positive return, they're Hyperliquid, TRON, and BNB. And they're all doing a hundred plus million of revenue, buybacks, value accrual to token holders. So yes, while Solana is number one in users, number one in transactions, number one in developer growth, number one in application revenue—so the revenue that applications built on top of Solana get—uh, number one in trading volume across all the different chains, number one in new tokens that are being launched. And yes, Solana is super resilient. There's been no downtime in the last over 22 months. Uh, Solana has the highest actual transaction per second. These developer user metrics don't matter. Like Wall Street, buy-side, sell-side analysts, they don't care. Like it doesn't actually affect Solana's valuation. So here's the proposal of what we think solves this problem. We should position Solana as a marketplace. A marketplace between users that bring capital and financial applications. A marketplace just like Uber is a marketplace for riders and drivers. Riders measured as monthly consumers. The GMV equivalent for drivers is gross bookings for Uber. Just like Airbnb is a marketplace for hosts and guests. We measure GMV as gross bookings and guests as nights booked. Um, Solana is a marketplace for financial applications that developers build and users that bring capital. Institutions like BlackRock or a Phantom wallet user. And the core metric that matters is global transfer volume. The GMV equivalent for Solana. That's why we call Solana internet capital markets. Solana is a marketplace for capital. And these metrics look pretty good. If you compare them to like an Uber and Airbnb, these public companies, like these are all pretty solid metrics. And the best part of having this like trading volume, transfer volume as the core GMV metric that matters for Solana as an ecosystem is you could break it down into trading volume, so like perp DEX, spot DEX volume. You could have it broken down by payment volume with the Cash App, with the Western Union deal. You can actually model these things out over time based off of, hey, like Solana signed Western Union, signed Cash App, it's gonna bring this amount of TPV. Or you can break it out into like other volume, you know, RWA volume, DePIN volume, et cetera. And, uh, one of the challenges I got on Twitter from Mert actually was how do we model Solana to a thousand dollars per token? So with this marketplace framework, it's actually not crazy. If we assume Solana gets 5% of the entire tokenization market in 2030, which is BCG forecasts basically 9 trillion of assets will move on-chain in 2030. If Solana captures 5% of that, that's 450 billion of AUM that moves on-chain. And if we assume a single dollar that is on Solana moves roughly 60 times—we call it the velocity multiple. Uh, for reference today, TRON does about 90 times. So a given dollar of stablecoin on TRON, it moves around 90 times in a given year. You get 27 trillion of this GMV metric of transfer volume. And roughly if you take the 2024 take rate for Solana, so that's like fees spent, Jito tips, et cetera, that's 27 billion of revenue. And you can model out incentives, OpEx, other pieces, and you can get to 20 billion of net income. With a 50 times price to earnings multiple, you can get to a trillion dollar market cap and a thousand dollar token. This is what Wall Street wants. This is what all the investment banks we work with, all the buy-side funds, all the asset managers that talk to us want. They want a simple model, a simple framework of, hey, AUM, let's track the amount of capital that's on the chain. Transfer volume, where's it coming from? Is it payments? Is it trading? Is it from RWAs? And what is the take rate? What is the ultimate revenue? This is what Wall Street wants. And a lot of the asset managers, a lot of our customers, we share this model and it's highly resonant. Um, a lot of them come to us and they're like, we just want like a free cash flow, like an earnings, a revenue number that we can write a report on and convince additional hedge funds and asset managers to buy their, uh, to buy SOL. And so this model is highly resonant with Wall Street. I think there's basically two paths forward for Solana. Solana can continue to trade based off flows, like oil and gas, as a commodity. Or Solana can trade like a high-growth technology business like Robinhood, which trades on earnings, trades on 74 times earnings. At Artemis, we strongly believe the world is shifting towards fundamentals. We stand for fundamentals and we work with some of the most prominent chains and protocols to help describe what are the metrics that matter to Wall Street, to the biggest asset managers, to the biggest liquid token funds. If this model resonated with you, that's the QR code. Feel free to rip it apart, uh, flame me on Twitter. Um, and if this general methodology of like fundamentals matter, here's how you should value, uh, blockchains resonates, would love to chat. Thanks so much.

enrichment.generated_query: Artemis Solana Breakpoint 2025 (Official Announcement Blog OR Technical Docs)

tags.impact_reason: The keynote outlines a crucial valuation framework for attracting institutional capital to Solana by prioritizing revenue over vanity metrics, but remains a strategic presentation rather than a direct protocol advancement.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a futuristic, monolithic stock ticker as the Semantic Anchor because the story is about applying traditional Wall Street valuation models ('Bloomberg', 'CapIQ', 'What Wall Street Wants') to a new digital asset, Solana. The ticker physically represents this bridge between old finance and new technology. Instead of paper, it extrudes a crystalline data ribbon, symbolizing the transformation of a 'commodity' into a structured, high-value tech asset. I included the text '$1,000' as a crisp LED display on the machine, as this is the specific, high-impact price target from the headline that anchors the entire valuation argument.
visual_idea.description: A macro, detailed shot focusing on the point of creation. We see the glowing, crystalline data ribbon being extruded from the seamless, polished obsidian and chrome slot of the financial machine. Light refracts through the crystal's facets, illuminating the complex, lattice-like data structures frozen within. The image captures the textural contrast between the cold, perfect smoothness of the machine and the intricate, energetic light of the data-infused crystal.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro, hyper-detailed shot, perfectly composed for a 1:1 square aspect ratio, focusing symmetrically on the point of creation. The frame is filled with the dramatic extrusion of a glowing, crystalline data ribbon from a seamless, highly polished obsidian and chrome slot of a futuristic financial machine. High-contrast, cinematic lighting illuminates the scene, casting sharp specular highlights across the mirror-like polished obsidian and chrome surfaces, emphasizing their cold, perfect smoothness. Intense light refracts through every facet of the emergent crystal, revealing the complex, intricate, lattice-like data structures frozen within with energetic light. The radiant glow of the data ribbon creates dramatic, deep shadows and highlights, enhancing the textural contrast between the machine's sleek precision and the vibrant, data-infused crystal. The composition is a tight macro, showcasing every minute detail and filling the entire frame with rich textures and light play. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic lighting, high dynamic range.

editorial.headline: Artemis Unveils $1,000 SOL Model via 'Internet Capital Markets' Framework
editorial.standfirst: Wall Street rejects 'commodity' valuations for Layer 1s. To unlock a $1 trillion market cap, Artemis argues Solana must be priced as a marketplace for global transfer volume.
key_takeaways: Artemis (backing Fidelity, VanEck) claims TVL and DAU metrics are irrelevant for institutional valuation.
New 'Marketplace' framework targets $20B Net Income and $1,000 SOL by capturing 5% of tokenized assets.
Valuation hinges on 'Global Transfer Volume' replacing TVL, applying a 50x P/E multiple to transaction fees.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
For years, the crypto industry has pitched itself to institutions using metrics that simply do not translate to traditional finance. According to **Jon Ma**, CEO of institutional data platform **Artemis**, Solana currently trades like "oil and gas"—a commodity driven by flows, supply, and demand. This is a fatal bottleneck for valuation. Wall Street does not ascribe high-growth multiples to commodities; they ascribe them to technology businesses with clear earnings. Ma argues that for Solana to break its current ceiling, it must stop being valued like a barrel of oil and start being valued like **Robinhood** or **Uber**.

 The pivot requires a complete overhaul of the metrics reported to asset managers. **Artemis**, whose data powers heavyweights like **Fidelity**, **Franklin Templeton**, and **VanEck**, has found that traditional "vanity metrics"—Total Value Locked (TVL), daily active addresses, and developer counts—have zero correlation with institutional capital allocation. Instead, Ma proposes positioning Solana as a **"Marketplace for Capital,"** comparable to how Airbnb is a marketplace for space. In this model, the primary metric is not TVL, but **Global Transfer Volume**—the GMV equivalent of capital moving through the network's financial applications.

 The math behind this framework offers a concrete path to a $1,000 SOL token. Citing **BCG's** forecast that $9 trillion in assets will move on-chain by 2030, Ma's model assumes Solana captures just 5% of this market ($450 billion). Crucially, this capital is not stagnant; applying a "velocity multiple" of 60x—conservative compared to TRON's current 90x—yields $27 trillion in annual transfer volume. If Solana maintains its 2024 take rate on this volume, the network generates **$27 billion in revenue** and roughly **$20 billion in net income**. At a 50x price-to-earnings (P/E) multiple, this fundamentally justifies a $1 trillion market cap.

 This is the "Alpha" that Wall Street has been waiting for: a standardized earnings model that fits into an Excel spreadsheet. The "Internet Capital Markets" thesis moves the conversation away from abstract decentralization ethos toward tangible cash flows derived from payments (Cash App, Western Union) and trading volume. As Ma bluntly put it to the audience in Abu Dhabi, institutions do not care about developer commits; they care about P/E ratios. If Solana can successfully transition its narrative from a commodity blockchain to a high-growth marketplace, the fundamentals finally support a four-figure token price.
